title: How to Speed Up a Slow Windows PC
content:
# How to Speed Up a Slow Windows PC

A slow Windows PC is one of the most common tech complaints. The good news is that most slowdowns can be fixed without spending money. Here is a step-by-step guide.

## Step 1: Disable Unnecessary Startup Programs

Many apps launch automatically when Windows starts, hogging RAM and CPU before you even open a browser.

1. Press **Ctrl + Shift + Esc** to open Task Manager
2. Click the **Startup** tab
3. Right-click any app you don't need at startup and select **Disable**
4. Focus on apps with **High** startup impact

Common culprits: Spotify, Discord, OneDrive, Teams, Adobe updaters.

## Step 2: Free Up Disk Space

Windows slows down when your drive is more than 85% full.

1. Press **Win + R**, type `cleanmgr`, press Enter
2. Select your C: drive and click OK
3. Check all boxes and click **Clean up system files**
4. Also delete contents of `C:\Windows\Temp` manually

For deeper cleaning, use **WinDirStat** (free) to find large files wasting space.

## Step 3: Scan for Malware

Malware often runs silently in the background, slowing your PC and stealing data.

1. Open **Windows Security** (search in Start menu)
2. Go to **Virus & threat protection**
3. Click **Quick scan** — if issues found, run a **Full scan**
4. For a second opinion, download **Malwarebytes Free** and run a scan

## Step 4: Update Windows and Drivers

Outdated drivers, especially GPU and chipset drivers, cause performance issues.

1. Go to **Settings → Windows Update → Check for updates**
2. For GPU drivers: visit nvidia.com, amd.com, or intel.com and download the latest
3. For general driver updates, use **Device Manager** (right-click Start → Device Manager)

## Step 5: Adjust Visual Effects

Windows animations look nice but consume resources on older PCs.

1. Search for **Adjust the appearance and performance of Windows**
2. Select **Adjust for best performance**, or manually uncheck effects you don't need

## Step 6: Check RAM Usage

1. Open Task Manager → **Performance** tab → Memory
2. If memory usage is consistently above 80%, consider upgrading RAM
3. 8 GB is the minimum for smooth Windows 11 use; 16 GB is recommended

## Step 7: Consider a Clean Boot

If the PC is still slow, a clean boot helps identify software conflicts.

1. Press **Win + R**, type `msconfig`, press Enter
2. Under **Services**, check **Hide all Microsoft services**, then click **Disable all**
3. Under **Startup**, click **Open Task Manager** and disable all items
4. Restart and test — if faster, re-enable services one by one to find the culprit

## Quick Diagnostic Command

```
msinfo32
```

This opens System Information — useful for checking your hardware specs and identifying bottlenecks.

**Next step:** Start with Step 1 (startup programs) — it takes 5 minutes and often makes an immediate difference.

title: How to Speed Up a Slow Mac
content:
# How to Speed Up a Slow Mac

Macs are known for their reliability, but they can slow down over time. Here's how to get yours running fast again.

## Step 1: Check What's Consuming Resources

1. Open **Activity Monitor** (search in Spotlight with Cmd+Space)
2. Click the **CPU** tab — sort by CPU % to find resource-hungry apps
3. Click the **Memory** tab — check Memory Pressure at the bottom
4. If Memory Pressure is consistently yellow or red, you need more RAM or to close apps

## Step 2: Free Up Storage

MacOS needs at least 10-15% free disk space to run well.

1. Click **Apple menu → About This Mac → Storage → Manage**
2. Use **Recommendations** — enable Optimise Storage, Empty Trash Automatically
3. Review **Large Files** and delete anything you no longer need
4. Empty the Trash: right-click Trash icon → Empty Trash

## Step 3: Manage Login Items

1. Go to **System Settings → General → Login Items**
2. Remove apps you don't need at startup by selecting them and clicking the minus (−) button

## Step 4: Update macOS and Apps

1. **Apple menu → System Settings → General → Software Update**
2. Install all available updates — they often include performance improvements
3. Update App Store apps: open App Store → Updates tab

## Step 5: Reset SMC and PRAM (Older Macs)

For Intel Macs, resetting the System Management Controller can fix performance issues.

**Resetting NVRAM (Intel Mac):**
1. Shut down your Mac
2. Press power, then immediately hold **Option + Command + P + R**
3. Hold for 20 seconds, then release

**For Apple Silicon Macs (M1/M2/M3):** NVRAM resets automatically, so this step isn't needed.

## Step 6: Reduce Visual Effects

1. Go to **System Settings → Accessibility → Display**
2. Enable **Reduce Motion** and **Reduce Transparency**

## Step 7: Rebuild Spotlight Index

A corrupted Spotlight index can cause high CPU usage.

1. **System Settings → Siri & Spotlight → Spotlight Privacy**
2. Add your main drive to the Privacy list, wait a moment, then remove it
3. This forces Spotlight to rebuild its index

## Step 8: Check for Malware

Macs can get malware too. Run a free scan with **Malwarebytes for Mac** to check.

**Next step:** Open Activity Monitor right now and check which app is consuming the most CPU — that's your immediate culprit.

title: How to Identify a Phishing Email
content:
# How to Identify a Phishing Email

Phishing emails are fake messages designed to trick you into giving up passwords, banking details, or installing malware. In India, phishing attacks often impersonate HDFC Bank, SBI, IRCTC, Income Tax Department, and popular delivery services.

## The 7 Red Flags of Phishing Emails

### 1. Urgent or Threatening Language
Phrases like "Your account will be suspended in 24 hours," "Immediate action required," or "Final warning" are classic phishing tactics. Legitimate companies rarely use this tone.

### 2. Suspicious Sender Address
Look carefully at the actual email address (not just the display name):
- Legitimate: `noreply@hdfcbank.com`
- Phishing: `noreply@hdfc-bank-secure.com` or `hdfcbank@gmail.com`

Always click on the sender name to reveal the full email address.

### 3. Generic Greetings
"Dear Customer" or "Dear User" instead of your actual name. Legitimate companies you have accounts with usually address you by name.

### 4. Suspicious Links
Hover your mouse over any link (don't click) to see where it actually goes. Watch for:
- Misspelled domains: `amazzon.com`, `paypa1.com`
- Extra subdomains: `sbi.com.secure-login.xyz`
- Random strings: `bit.ly/xk93ms`

### 5. Unexpected Attachments
Never open attachments from unexpected emails, especially `.exe`, `.zip`, `.pdf`, or `.doc` files. These can install malware.

### 6. Requests for Sensitive Information
Legitimate organisations never ask for passwords, OTPs, Aadhaar numbers, PAN numbers, CVV codes, or PINs via email.

### 7. Poor Grammar and Spelling
Many phishing emails (especially those originating internationally) contain grammatical errors, awkward phrasing, or odd formatting.

## What to Do With a Suspicious Email

1. **Do not click any links** or download attachments
2. **Do not reply** — even replies can confirm your email is active
3. **Report it** as phishing/spam in your email client (Gmail: three dots → Report phishing)
4. **Verify independently** — if it claims to be your bank, call the official number on the back of your card
5. **Delete it**

## Reporting Phishing in India

- Report to your bank's official email/phone
- Report cybercrime at **cybercrime.gov.in**
- Helpline: **1930** (Cyber Crime Helpline)

**Next step:** Check your last 5 emails — look at the sender's actual email address for each one. You may be surprised what you find.

title: What to Do If You Clicked a Phishing Link
content:
# What to Do If You Clicked a Phishing Link

Don't panic — acting quickly can contain most damage from a phishing link. Follow these steps immediately.

## Step 1: Disconnect from the Internet (Do This First)

Turn off your Wi-Fi and unplug your ethernet cable. This stops malware from sending data to attackers and prevents it from downloading additional malicious files.

## Step 2: Don't Enter Any Information

If the link opened a website, close it immediately. Do NOT enter any username, password, OTP, Aadhaar, PAN, or banking details — even if the page looks legitimate.

## Step 3: Run a Full Malware Scan

1. Reconnect to the internet briefly to update your antivirus definitions
2. Disconnect again and run a **full system scan** with Windows Defender or your antivirus
3. Download and run **Malwarebytes Free** for a second opinion
4. Quarantine and delete any threats found

## Step 4: Change Your Passwords Immediately

Change passwords for any account you were logged into or recently used. Prioritise:
1. **Email** (most important — controls password resets for everything else)
2. **Banking and UPI apps** (PhonePe, Paytm, Google Pay, net banking)
3. **Social media** (Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp)
4. **Work accounts**

Use a different device if possible when changing passwords.

## Step 5: Enable Two-Factor Authentication (2FA)

After changing passwords, enable 2FA on all critical accounts. This means even if an attacker has your password, they cannot log in without your phone.

## Step 6: Check Your Accounts for Suspicious Activity

- Look for login alerts in your email (Gmail: Last account activity at the bottom)
- Check bank statements and UPI transaction history
- Review your email's Sent folder for messages you didn't send
- Check connected apps/devices in your Google/Apple account settings

## Step 7: Monitor for the Next Few Weeks

- Watch for unusual SMS messages or OTPs you didn't request
- Monitor your bank account for small test transactions (attackers sometimes test with ₹1-2 before larger fraud)
- Consider placing a fraud alert with your bank

## If Financial Fraud Occurred

1. Call your bank immediately — most Indian banks have a 24/7 fraud helpline
2. Call **1930** (India Cyber Crime Helpline) within 24 hours for the best chance of recovery
3. File a complaint at **cybercrime.gov.in**
4. Time is critical — quick action can freeze fraudulent transactions

**Next step:** Change your email password right now, even if you're not sure you entered anything — it's the highest-value account to protect.

title: How to Create Strong Passwords and Use a Password Manager
content:
# How to Create Strong Passwords and Use a Password Manager

Weak and reused passwords are the #1 cause of account breaches. A password manager solves both problems.

## What Makes a Password Strong?

A strong password has three properties:
1. **Long** — at least 12 characters, ideally 16+
2. **Random** — not based on dictionary words, names, or dates
3. **Unique** — never reused across different accounts

**Example of a strong password:** `T7#mK9@pLq2!nVx8`

**Passwords to avoid:**
- `password123` (too common)
- `Priya@1995` (contains personal info)
- The same password on multiple sites

## Using a Passphrase Instead

A passphrase is easier to remember but equally strong: combine 4-5 random words.

Example: `correct-horse-battery-staple-42` (19 characters, very hard to crack)

## Why You Need a Password Manager

The average person has 90+ online accounts. It's impossible to memorise unique strong passwords for all of them. A password manager:
- **Generates** strong random passwords automatically
- **Stores** them securely with AES-256 encryption
- **Autofills** them on websites and apps
- Requires you to remember only **one master password**

## Recommended Free Password Managers

### Bitwarden (Best Free Option)
- Completely free for personal use
- Open source (code is publicly audited)
- Works on Android, iOS, Windows, Mac, browser extensions
- Download at: bitwarden.com

### Other Good Options
- **1Password** (paid, ₹300/month, very polished)
- **KeePass** (free, offline-only, more technical)

## Getting Started with Bitwarden

1. Go to bitwarden.com → Create Account
2. Choose a strong master password — write it down and store it safely offline
3. Install the browser extension (Chrome, Firefox, Edge)
4. Install the mobile app on your phone
5. Start saving passwords — whenever you log in somewhere, Bitwarden offers to save it
6. For existing accounts, update passwords to strong generated ones one by one

## Protecting Your Master Password

Your master password is the key to everything:
- Never store it digitally
- Write it down and keep it in a safe physical location
- Enable 2FA on your Bitwarden account itself as backup

**Next step:** Download Bitwarden right now and set it up. Then change your email account password to a strong generated one — this is your most important account.

title: How to Set Up Two-Factor Authentication (2FA)
content:
# How to Set Up Two-Factor Authentication (2FA)

Two-factor authentication (2FA) adds a second layer of security beyond your password. Even if an attacker knows your password, they cannot access your account without the second factor.

## How 2FA Works

After entering your password, you must also provide:
- A **time-based one-time code** (TOTP) from an authenticator app — changes every 30 seconds
- An **SMS OTP** sent to your phone number (less secure but better than nothing)
- A **hardware key** like a YubiKey (most secure)

## Why 2FA Is Critical

Passwords get stolen through data breaches, phishing, and keyloggers. 2FA means a stolen password alone is useless to an attacker.

## Best Authenticator Apps

### Google Authenticator (Recommended for Beginners)
- Free, easy to use
- Available on Android and iOS
- Generates TOTP codes offline

### Authy (Better for Backup)
- Free
- Supports encrypted cloud backup (so you don't lose access if you lose your phone)
- Works on Android, iOS, and desktop

## Setting Up 2FA: Step by Step

### On Google/Gmail:
1. Go to myaccount.google.com
2. Click **Security** → **How you sign in to Google**
3. Click **2-Step Verification** → **Get started**
4. Follow prompts — choose **Authenticator app** for best security
5. Scan the QR code with your authenticator app
6. Enter the 6-digit code to confirm

### On Instagram:
1. Profile → Menu (three lines) → **Settings** → **Security**
2. **Two-Factor Authentication** → **Get Started**
3. Choose **Authentication App** → scan QR code

### On WhatsApp:
1. Settings → **Account** → **Two-step verification**
2. **Enable** → set a 6-digit PIN
3. Add a recovery email address

## Save Your Backup Codes

When enabling 2FA, services provide backup codes. These let you log in if you lose your phone.
- **Download or print them**
- Store them somewhere safe, separate from your phone
- Never store them only in the cloud (that defeats the purpose)

## Which Accounts to Protect First

1. Email (Gmail, Outlook) — highest priority
2. Banking and UPI (net banking, PhonePe, Paytm)
3. Social media (Instagram, Facebook, Twitter/X)
4. Password manager account
5. Work email and productivity tools

**Next step:** Enable 2FA on your Gmail or primary email account right now — it takes under 5 minutes and dramatically reduces your risk.

title: How to Check If Your Email Has Been in a Data Breach
content:
# How to Check If Your Email Has Been in a Data Breach

Data breaches happen when companies' databases are hacked and user information is stolen. Your email address and passwords may be circulating on the dark web without your knowledge.

## What Is a Data Breach?

When a website or service you use is hacked, attackers may steal:
- Email addresses and passwords
- Phone numbers
- Names and addresses
- Credit card information
- Security question answers

This stolen data is often sold on the dark web or used for targeted attacks.

## How to Check: Have I Been Pwned (HIBP)

The most trusted free service is **Have I Been Pwned** (haveibeenpwned.com), created by security researcher Troy Hunt. It contains data from thousands of breaches.

1. Go to **haveibeenpwned.com**
2. Enter your email address
3. Click **pwned?**
4. If it shows red — your email was found in one or more breaches
5. Scroll down to see which breaches and what data was exposed

**Is this safe to use?** Yes — HIBP only checks if your email was in a breach. It doesn't store your search or require you to log in.

## What to Do If You're Breached

### If only your email was exposed:
- Low risk — monitor for phishing emails targeting you

### If your password was exposed:
1. **Change the password immediately** on that site
2. Check if you used the same password on other sites — change those too
3. Enable 2FA on the affected account

### If your phone number was exposed:
- Be alert for SIM swap attempts and vishing (voice phishing) calls

### If financial data was exposed:
- Contact your bank immediately
- Monitor statements for suspicious transactions
- Consider setting a transaction limit via your bank app

## Setting Up Breach Alerts

HIBP offers free email notifications — you'll be alerted whenever your email appears in a new breach.

1. Go to haveibeenpwned.com → **Notify me**
2. Enter your email and click **Notify me of pwnage**
3. Verify via the confirmation email

## Checking Your Password Specifically

HIBP also has a **Passwords** checker (passwords.haveibeenpwned.com) that uses a clever k-anonymity technique — only the first 5 characters of your password hash are sent, so your actual password is never exposed.

**Next step:** Go to haveibeenpwned.com right now and check your primary email address. If found in breaches, change those passwords today.

title: WiFi Troubleshooting: Common Fixes for Internet Connectivity
content:
# WiFi Troubleshooting: Common Fixes for Internet Connectivity

Internet connectivity issues are frustrating but usually fixable without calling your ISP. Work through these steps in order.

## Step 1: Check If It's Just Your Device

Check if other devices (phone, tablet, other laptop) connect fine.
- **Only one device affected** → problem is with that device
- **All devices affected** → problem is with router or ISP

## Step 2: The Classic Restart

**For router/modem issues:**
1. Unplug the router and modem from power
2. Wait **30 seconds** (important — capacitors need to discharge)
3. Plug the modem back in first, wait 60 seconds until lights stabilise
4. Plug the router in, wait another 60 seconds
5. Test connection

This fixes about 70% of home WiFi issues.

## Step 3: Check for ISP Outages

Before troubleshooting further, check if your ISP has an outage:
- Jio: Check Jio.com or call 199
- Airtel: Check airtel.in or call 121
- BSNL: Call 1800-180-1503
- ACT Fibernet: Check actcorp.in
- Use Downdetector (downdetector.in) to see if others are reporting issues

## Step 4: Forget and Reconnect to WiFi

**On Windows:**
1. Click WiFi icon → the network → **Forget**
2. Reconnect and re-enter the password

**On Android:**
1. Settings → WiFi → Long press the network → **Forget**
2. Reconnect

## Step 5: Flush DNS Cache (Windows)

A corrupted DNS cache can block certain websites even when internet works.

Open Command Prompt as Administrator and run:
```
ipconfig /flushdns
ipconfig /release
ipconfig /renew
```

## Step 6: Check Router Placement

- Keep router in a central location, elevated off the floor
- Avoid placing near microwaves, cordless phones, or thick concrete walls
- 2.4 GHz band has better range but is slower; 5 GHz is faster but shorter range
- For better range, consider a WiFi extender or mesh system

## Step 7: Check for WiFi Congestion

In apartments, many networks on the same channel cause interference.

1. Log into your router (usually 192.168.1.1 or 192.168.0.1 in a browser)
2. Find the WiFi channel settings
3. Change from Auto to a specific channel: 1, 6, or 11 for 2.4 GHz

## Step 8: Update Router Firmware

Log into your router admin panel and check for firmware updates. Outdated firmware can cause connectivity and security issues.

## When to Call Your ISP

- All steps above fail and all devices are affected
- Router lights show no internet (WAN light is red or off)
- Internet works but speed is consistently far below your plan speed

**Next step:** Check if other devices are affected — this tells you immediately whether the problem is the device or the network.

title: Signs Your Computer Has Malware and How to Remove It
content:
# Signs Your Computer Has Malware and How to Remove It

Malware (malicious software) includes viruses, spyware, ransomware, adware, and trojans. Here's how to detect and remove it.

## Warning Signs of Malware Infection

### Performance Signs
- Computer suddenly became very slow
- Fan runs constantly even when you're not doing much
- Programs crash frequently or take forever to open
- Computer restarts randomly

### Browser Signs
- Homepage changed without your permission
- New toolbars or extensions you didn't install
- Constant pop-up ads, even on sites that don't usually have them
- Redirected to different websites when clicking links
- Search engine changed (e.g., suddenly using a different search engine)

### System Signs
- Antivirus is disabled or won't turn on
- Task Manager or Registry Editor won't open
- Unfamiliar programs in your Start menu or taskbar
- Hard drive light constantly active even when idle
- High network activity when you're not browsing

### Account Signs
- Friends receive spam from your email or social media
- Password doesn't work even though you didn't change it
- Strange sent emails or social media posts you didn't make

## How to Remove Malware

### Step 1: Boot Into Safe Mode
Safe Mode prevents most malware from loading.

**Windows:**
1. Hold Shift and click Restart
2. Go to Troubleshoot → Advanced Options → Startup Settings → Restart
3. Press **4** for Safe Mode

### Step 2: Run Windows Defender Full Scan
1. Open Windows Security → Virus & threat protection
2. Scan options → **Full scan** → Scan now
3. This can take 1-2 hours — let it complete

### Step 3: Run Malwarebytes
1. Download **Malwarebytes Free** from malwarebytes.com
2. Install and run a **Threat Scan**
3. Quarantine all detected items
4. Restart your computer

### Step 4: Run AdwCleaner (for Adware)
For browser redirects and unwanted toolbars, AdwCleaner (free from Malwarebytes) is very effective.

### Step 5: Check Browser Extensions
1. Open browser settings → Extensions
2. Remove any you don't recognise or didn't intentionally install

## If Ransomware Is Detected

Ransomware encrypts your files and demands payment. **Do not pay** — there's no guarantee you'll get your files back.
1. Disconnect from internet immediately
2. Do not restart your PC
3. Check **nomoreransom.org** — free decryption tools are available for many ransomware types
4. Contact a cybersecurity professional

## After Removal: Protect Yourself

1. Change all passwords from a clean device
2. Enable 2FA on important accounts
3. Keep Windows and all software updated
4. Never download software from unofficial sources
5. Be cautious with email attachments and links

**Next step:** Open Windows Security right now and check when your last scan was. If it's been more than a week, run a Quick Scan.

title: How to Set Up a VPN on Your Phone
content:
# How to Set Up a VPN on Your Phone

A VPN (Virtual Private Network) encrypts your internet connection, protecting your privacy especially on public WiFi. It hides your browsing from your ISP and prevents attackers on the same network from intercepting your data.

## When You Need a VPN

- **Public WiFi** (airports, cafes, hotels, malls) — most important use case
- Accessing work resources remotely
- Protecting privacy from ISP tracking
- Accessing geo-restricted content

## When a VPN Is NOT Enough

A VPN does NOT protect you from:
- Malware on your device
- Phishing attacks
- Weak passwords
- Data you voluntarily share with apps/websites

## Recommended VPN Apps

### Free Options
- **Proton VPN Free** — No data limit, no logs, based in Switzerland. Best free option.
- **Windscribe Free** — 10 GB/month free

### Paid Options (Better Performance)
- **NordVPN** (₹200-350/month on annual plan)
- **ExpressVPN** (₹500-600/month)
- **Proton VPN Plus** (₹350/month)

**Avoid free VPNs you haven't researched** — many sell your browsing data, defeating the purpose.

## Setting Up Proton VPN (Android)

1. Open **Google Play Store**
2. Search for **Proton VPN** → Install
3. Open the app → Sign up for a free account
4. Tap **Quick Connect** — Proton VPN will connect to the fastest available server
5. Look for the key icon in your status bar — this confirms VPN is active

## Setting Up Proton VPN (iPhone/iPad)

1. Open **App Store** → Search **Proton VPN** → Install
2. Open app → Create account or sign in
3. Tap **Quick Connect**
4. Allow VPN configuration when prompted — tap **Allow**
5. VPN icon (key symbol) appears in the status bar when connected

## Using a VPN Safely

- **Always connect** before using public WiFi
- The free Proton VPN plan has no data limit — leave it on whenever you're not home
- Some banking apps may block VPN connections — temporarily disconnect if your bank app stops working
- Your ISP can see you're using a VPN but not what you're doing

## Built-in VPN Options on Android

Many Android phones (especially Samsung) have a built-in VPN client under Settings → Connections → VPN. This requires manual server configuration — use a VPN provider that offers IKEv2 or OpenVPN configuration files.

**Next step:** Download Proton VPN right now and connect to it the next time you use public WiFi. The free plan has no data limit — there's no reason not to use it.

title: Safe Online Shopping: How to Avoid Scams
content:
# Safe Online Shopping: How to Avoid Scams

Online shopping fraud costs Indians crores of rupees every year. Most scams are avoidable with a few precautions.

## Stick to Trusted Platforms

Buy from established platforms you recognise: Amazon, Flipkart, Myntra, Nykaa, Meesho, BigBasket, or the brand's official website.

**Red flags for fake websites:**
- Prices that are impossibly low (80-90% off genuine brands)
- No physical address or customer care number
- Only WhatsApp or email for contact
- URL doesn't match the brand name (e.g., `flipkart-offers.net`)
- No HTTPS (padlock icon) in the browser address bar

## How to Verify a Website Is Legitimate

1. Check the URL carefully — scammers use misspellings: `amaz0n.in`, `flipkart-sale.com`
2. Look for the padlock (🔒) in the address bar — indicates HTTPS encryption
3. Search for reviews: "[website name] review" or "[website name] scam"
4. Check when the domain was registered at whois.domaintools.com — new domains (under 1 year old) for established-seeming brands are suspicious
5. Look for a valid GST number on Indian e-commerce sites

## Safe Payment Practices

- **Prefer UPI or net banking** over sharing card details with unknown sites
- **Never save card details** on unfamiliar websites
- Use **virtual cards** — many banks (HDFC, ICICI, Axis) offer virtual debit/credit cards for online transactions with a customisable limit
- **Avoid paying in advance** to unknown sellers, especially for high-value items
- **Cash on Delivery** is safer for new vendors

## Spotting Fake Deals and Offers

- Verify sale prices by checking the same product on official platforms
- "Limited time offers" that create urgency are a common manipulation tactic
- Lottery winnings, lucky draws, and cashback offers requiring you to pay first are always scams
- Unsolicited WhatsApp messages about deals are almost always fraud

## After a Suspicious Purchase

If you suspect you've been defrauded:
1. Call your bank immediately to block your card and dispute the transaction
2. File a complaint at **cybercrime.gov.in** or call **1930**
3. Report the website to Google Safe Browsing: safebrowsing.google.com/safebrowsing/report_phish/

## Social Media Shopping

Facebook and Instagram shops are common fraud vectors:
- Verify the seller has genuine reviews (look for review patterns — bulk 5-star reviews on the same day are fake)
- Use the platform's buyer protection if available
- Never pay outside the platform via UPI to unknown sellers

**Next step:** Before your next online purchase from an unfamiliar site, check it on Google with "[site name] reviews scam" — 5 minutes of research can save you thousands of rupees.

title: How to Secure Your Home WiFi Router
content:
# How to Secure Your Home WiFi Router

Your home router is the gateway to all your internet traffic. An unsecured router lets neighbours steal your bandwidth, hackers attack your devices, and ISPs monitor everything you do. Here's how to lock it down.

## Step 1: Change the Default Router Password

Most routers ship with default admin credentials like `admin/admin` or `admin/password`. These are publicly listed and anyone on your network can access your router settings.

1. Open a browser and go to your router's admin panel:
   - Most common: **192.168.1.1** or **192.168.0.1**
   - Check the sticker on the bottom of your router
2. Log in with current credentials (often on the sticker)
3. Find **Administration** or **System** settings
4. Change the **admin password** to a strong, unique password
5. Save and note it down

## Step 2: Use Strong WiFi Encryption (WPA3 or WPA2)

1. In router settings, find **Wireless** or **WiFi Settings**
2. Under **Security Mode**, select **WPA3** if available, otherwise **WPA2-AES**
3. **Never use WEP or WPA** — these are crackable in minutes

## Step 3: Set a Strong WiFi Password

- Use at least 12 characters with letters, numbers, and symbols
- Don't use your name, address, or phone number
- Change it periodically, especially if you've shared it widely

## Step 4: Disable WPS (WiFi Protected Setup)

WPS has known security vulnerabilities that allow attackers to crack your WiFi password.

1. Find **WPS** settings in your router panel
2. Turn it **Off**

## Step 5: Disable Remote Management

Remote management allows your router to be accessed from outside your home — a security risk.

1. Find **Remote Management** or **Remote Access** in settings
2. Disable it unless you specifically need it

## Step 6: Enable the Router's Firewall

1. Look for **Firewall** or **Security** in your router settings
2. Enable it — it blocks unsolicited incoming connections

## Step 7: Update Router Firmware

Router firmware updates fix security vulnerabilities.

1. Find **Firmware Update** or **Software Update** in settings
2. Check for and install updates
3. Enable automatic updates if available

## Step 8: Create a Guest Network

For visitors and IoT devices (smart TVs, bulbs, cameras), create a separate guest network.

1. Find **Guest Network** in WiFi settings
2. Enable it with a different password
3. Enable **Guest Isolation** — this prevents guests from accessing devices on your main network

## Step 9: Check Connected Devices

Regularly check which devices are connected to your network.

1. Look for **DHCP Client List** or **Connected Devices** in router settings
2. If you see unknown devices, change your WiFi password immediately

**Next step:** Log into your router admin panel today and change the default admin password if you haven't already. This single step prevents the most common home network attacks.

title: What to Do When Your Phone Is Running Out of Storage
content:
# What to Do When Your Phone Is Running Out of Storage

Running out of storage slows your phone, stops app updates, and prevents taking photos. Here's how to free up space on both Android and iPhone.

## Android: Free Up Storage

### Step 1: Use Google Files (Best Free Tool)
1. Download **Files by Google** from the Play Store if not pre-installed
2. Open it — it automatically identifies junk files, duplicate photos, large files, and unused apps
3. Tap **Clean** — it safely removes identified junk

### Step 2: Clear App Cache
App cache builds up over time. Each app stores temporary data that can be safely deleted.

1. Go to **Settings → Apps**
2. Tap each large app (WhatsApp, Instagram, YouTube, Chrome)
3. Tap **Storage → Clear Cache** (not Clear Data — that resets the app)

WhatsApp alone often accumulates gigabytes of media. Consider:
- Settings → Chats → Chat Backup → Manage Storage

### Step 3: Move Photos to Cloud
1. Install **Google Photos** and enable backup
2. Once backed up, tap **Free up space** to remove local copies
3. You get 15 GB free with Google (shared with Gmail and Drive)

### Step 4: Move Apps to SD Card
If your phone has a microSD card slot:
1. Settings → Apps → select an app → Storage → Change (if supported)
2. Move to SD Card

Note: Not all apps support this; system apps cannot be moved.

### Step 5: Uninstall Unused Apps
1. Go to Settings → Apps
2. Sort by size — remove apps you haven't opened in months

## iPhone: Free Up Storage

### Step 1: Check What's Using Space
1. Settings → General → iPhone Storage
2. Wait for the list to load — it shows exactly what's consuming space

### Step 2: Enable iCloud Photos
1. Settings → Photos → iCloud Photos → Enable
2. Choose **Optimise iPhone Storage** — full-resolution photos stay in iCloud; smaller previews on device

### Step 3: Delete Large Apps
In Settings → General → iPhone Storage, tap any app to see its size and option to offload it (keeps data, removes app) or delete entirely.

### Step 4: Clear Safari Cache
Settings → Safari → Clear History and Website Data

### Step 5: Delete Old Messages and Attachments
Messages → Settings → Keep Messages → change from Forever to **30 Days** or **1 Year**

## How to Prevent Storage Issues

- Enable auto-backup for photos immediately so you never need them locally
- Regularly review and delete WhatsApp media (the biggest culprit)
- Use streaming apps (Spotify, Netflix) instead of downloading

**Next step:** Open Files by Google (Android) or Settings → General → iPhone Storage (iPhone) right now to see your top space consumers — you'll likely find gigabytes you can free up in minutes.

title: Understanding Browser Privacy Settings
content:
# Understanding Browser Privacy Settings

Your browser is the window through which you access the internet — and through which companies track you. Here's how to take control of your privacy.

## What Browsers Track About You

- **Cookies**: Small files websites store to remember you, personalise content, and track you across sites
- **Browsing history**: Sites you've visited
- **Search history**: What you've searched for
- **Location**: If you've given permission
- **Fingerprint**: Unique combination of browser, OS, screen size, fonts — allows tracking without cookies

## Chrome Privacy Settings (Most Common)

### Essential Settings to Change

1. Open Chrome → three dots menu → **Settings**
2. **Privacy and security → Third-party cookies** → Select **Block third-party cookies**
3. **Privacy and security → Privacy Sandbox** → Turn off all advertising-related features
4. **Privacy and security → Clear browsing data** → Clear cookies and cache regularly
5. **Sync and Google services** → Turn off sync if you don't want Google seeing your browsing

### Chrome Privacy Shortcuts
- **Incognito mode** (Ctrl+Shift+N): Doesn't save browsing history locally. Does NOT make you anonymous to websites or your ISP.

## Firefox: A More Privacy-Focused Choice

Firefox blocks more trackers by default than Chrome:
1. Settings → **Privacy & Security** → Enhanced Tracking Protection → Select **Strict**
2. DNS over HTTPS: Settings → scroll down → Enable **DNS over HTTPS** → choose Cloudflare or NextDNS

## Essential Privacy Extensions

### uBlock Origin (Most Important)
- Blocks ads and trackers aggressively
- Free, open source
- Available for Chrome, Firefox, Edge
- After installing: click the icon → enable **uBlock Origin Lite** if it offers it

### Privacy Badger (from EFF)
- Automatically learns to block invisible trackers
- Good complement to uBlock Origin

## What Incognito Mode Actually Does

**Does:** Prevents browser from saving history, cookies, and form data locally on your device.

**Does NOT:**
- Hide your activity from your ISP
- Hide your activity from websites you visit
- Hide your activity from your employer (on work networks)
- Protect you from malware

## Google Account Privacy Settings

If you use a Google account in Chrome:
1. Go to **myaccount.google.com/data-and-privacy**
2. Under **History settings**: turn off Web & App Activity, YouTube History, Location History
3. **Ad settings**: turn off personalised ads

## Safe Browsing Settings

Make sure Safe Browsing is on — it warns you about dangerous sites:
- Chrome: Settings → Privacy and security → Security → **Enhanced protection**
- This checks URLs against Google's list of known malicious sites

**Next step:** Install uBlock Origin from the Chrome Web Store right now — it blocks thousands of trackers on every page you visit, requires no configuration, and makes browsing faster.

title: How to Recover a Hacked Social Media Account
content:
# How to Recover a Hacked Social Media Account

Having a social media account hacked is stressful, but recovery is usually possible if you act quickly. Here's what to do for each major platform.

## Immediate Steps (Do These First)

1. **Check your email** for security alerts from the platform — these often contain a link to reverse unauthorised changes
2. **Try to log in** — if successful, immediately change your password and enable 2FA
3. **Check connected devices** — look for unfamiliar logins and remove them

## Instagram Account Recovery

### If You Can Still Log In
1. Go to Settings → **Account Centre** → **Password and security**
2. Change password immediately
3. Check **Where you're logged in** → Remove unknown devices
4. Enable **Two-factor authentication**

### If the Hacker Changed Your Password
1. On the login screen, tap **Forgot password?**
2. Enter your email or phone number
3. Select **Get a login link** sent to your email or SMS
4. If the hacker changed your email too: tap **Need more help?** → follow the video selfie verification process
5. If all else fails: Use the **"I can't access this email or phone number"** option — Instagram may ask you to verify your identity with a photo ID

## Facebook Account Recovery

1. Go to **facebook.com/hacked**
2. Follow the Compromised Account flow
3. Facebook will walk you through securing your account
4. If locked out: **facebook.com/login/identify** → enter your email or phone
5. Check trusted contacts: Settings → Security → Trusted Contacts (if you set these up)

## WhatsApp Account Recovery

WhatsApp accounts are tied to your phone number — recovery is straightforward:
1. Re-install WhatsApp
2. Verify your phone number via SMS OTP
3. The hacker will be automatically logged out
4. Set up **Two-Step Verification**: Settings → Account → Two-step verification

**Note:** If you receive a WhatsApp OTP you didn't request, never share it. This is likely a SIM swap or account takeover attempt.

## Twitter/X Account Recovery

1. Click **Forgot password?** on login
2. Enter your email or phone number for a reset link
3. If email access is also compromised: use the **I don't have access to these** option
4. Submit a support request at help.twitter.com with proof of identity

## After Recovery: Secure Your Account

1. **Enable 2FA immediately** — this is the single most effective protection
2. **Change the password** on any other account that used the same password
3. **Check connected third-party apps** — revoke any you don't recognise
4. **Notify your followers** that your account was hacked (so they don't click malicious links sent by the hacker)
5. **Report to cybercrime.gov.in** if significant damage was done

## Preventing Future Hacks

- Use a unique, strong password for each account
- Enable 2FA using an authenticator app (more secure than SMS)
- Be suspicious of login notifications — verify every sign-in alert
- Never enter your social media credentials on third-party sites claiming to offer followers, analytics, or prizes

**Next step:** Enable 2FA on your most important social media account right now — it takes 5 minutes and makes account takeover nearly impossible even if your password is stolen.
